metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command (or three sub-commands) to AC3 that mechanically verify each sub-claim in rebar-debug/SKILL.md — e.g. `grep -q 'Design and prove a RED oracle' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`, `grep -q 'contract card' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`, and a grep for the deferred-to-shared-standard phrase in the LLM-surface paragraph.
findings.finding: AC3 bundles three distinct verifiable sub-claims for rebar-debug/SKILL.md (Step 4 retitled, LLM-surface paragraph updated to defer to shared standard, contract card added as fourth handoff item) but provides no grep/diff proving command for any of them — an executor could complete two of three changes with no failing check to catch the omission.
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to AC3:
```
Verify with:
  grep -q 'Design and prove a RED oracle' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md
  grep -q 'contract card' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md
  grep -q 'shared/test-design.md' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md  # already in AC2; kept for locality
```
(The exact strings can be adjusted to match the authored text, but the commands should be stated.)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a grep proving command to AC4, e.g. `grep -q 'shared/' examples/claude-skills/README.md`, to make the README update mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: AC4 (README documents the shared/ convention) has no concrete proving command — the completion verdict relies entirely on a human reading the file.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: `Verify with: grep -q 'shared/' examples/claude-skills/README.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2's verify command (or add a companion verify) to confirm the reference appears near the named integration point — e.g., use `grep -n` and document the expected line range, or add `grep -A2 -B2 'shared/test-design.md'` and state what surrounding text must be present.
findings.finding: AC2's verification command (`grep -l 'shared/test-design.md' ...`) can only confirm the string exists somewhere in each SKILL.md, but the AC text additionally claims 'each reference sitting at the integration point named above' — a placement guarantee the grep cannot check. The AC text makes a stronger claim than its verify step can satisfy.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or supplement the `grep -l` command with a check that also validates context: e.g., for rebar-debug verify that the reference appears within a few lines of 'Step 4' or 'Phase 2'; for rebar-implement within 'Phase 4a' or '4b'; for rebar-brainstorm within 'Before recording tickets'. A minimal approach: document in the AC that a human reviewer must confirm placement at the named section during review, making the verification requirement explicit even if not fully automatable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observable check to AC #3 confirming that rebar-debug Step 5's mutation check is updated to defect-seeded wording (e.g., 'Step 5 names defect-seeded mutation, not arbitrary negation').
findings.finding: AC #3 verifies rebar-debug's Step 4 retitling, LLM-eval paragraph update, and Step 5 contract-card handoff, but does NOT include an observable proof that Step 5's mutation check is updated to be defect-seeded — a named body goal that has no corresponding acceptance criterion check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #3; Plan body section 'Skill integration — rebar-debug'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #3: 'Step 5's mutation-check instruction names defect-seeded mutation (re-introduce the exact defect from the confirmed root cause), replacing any arbitrary-negation wording.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces LLM-surface eval requirements in test-design.md (pinned model/settings, paired before/after prompts, multiple samples with predeclared threshold, negative control, quantified failure rates) that conflict with the existing LLM-surface RED-artifact protocol in rebar-debug's Step 4 and llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (which only requires 'an eval or behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction'). The plan never states whether test-design.md supersedes, augments, or is reconciled with the existing llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 spec — leaving two co-resident specifications with divergent requirements for what constitutes a valid LLM-surface RED artifact.
findings.location: Plan section: 'LLM-surface evals' bullet; rebar-debug SKILL.md Step 4 (line 444–446); examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (lines 112–114)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's rebar-debug integration section, add: 'Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph is updated to cite test-design.md's LLM-surface eval requirements (pinned model, multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control) as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding the single-eval description currently in that paragraph and in llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the form of the shared-file reference for rebar-implement (e.g. 'add a load instruction at Phase 4a entry, parallel to rebar-debug's Phase 2 entry load') so the executor does not have to invent the integration pattern.
findings.finding: The rebar-implement integration description says only 'Phase 4a/4b reference the shared file for oracle design (inventory, tier, oracle completeness, gating proof); held-out structure unchanged' without specifying the form of the reference (a load instruction like the rebar-debug Phase 2 entry point, a new bullet, an inline sentence, a subsection). An executor must invent the structural form, which may produce an inconsistent integration compared to rebar-debug's precisely described changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the exact mechanism by which a SKILL.md instruction causes the executing agent to read `../shared/test-design.md` at runtime (e.g., an explicit `Read` tool call instruction in the SKILL.md prose, vs. bare naming), and confirm that `../` relative paths resolve correctly from a SKILL.md file's location in Claude Code's skill execution environment — citing the existing `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` same-directory precedent as the baseline to extend.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `../shared/test-design.md` 'resolves both in this repo and in a skills installation where `shared/` sits beside the skill directories,' but this cross-directory relative-path loading is an unverified capability claim. The only existing precedent for a supplementary reference file in a skill (`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` in rebar-debug) is referenced as 'in this skill's directory' — a same-directory path — not a `../` cross-directory path. The plan does not confirm that Claude Code's skill execution environment follows `../` relative paths from prose instructions in SKILL.md, nor specify the exact loading mechanism (an explicit tool call instruction vs. bare prose prose naming the path).
findings.location: Plan 'What' section, paragraph 2: 'each SKILL.md naming the path relative to its own directory (`../shared/test-design.md`) plus its repo location, so the reference resolves both in this repo and in a skills installation where `shared/` sits beside the skill directories'

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the rebar-debug Step 4 and Step 5 behavioral changes (e.g., 'revert commit X' or an expand-contract sequence where the new steps run alongside the old for a trial period before the old is removed).
findings.finding: The plan modifies existing, deployed behavior in rebar-debug (Step 4 retitled and flow changed, Step 5 mutation check changed to defect-seeded, fixer handoff list altered) without stating any rollback or back-out mechanism. Acknowledging the changes is not sufficient under T4 — the plan must also provide a reversibility path.
findings.location: Skill integration section — rebar-debug/SKILL.md changes; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rollback note to the plan: 'If the new Step 4/5 flow degrades debug outcomes, revert via `git revert <commit>` — the old SKILL.md is fully recoverable from git history.' Alternatively, stage the change as expand-contract: introduce the shared file and reference it as optional/supplementary in Step 4 first, then in a follow-on ticket make it the sole authority.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether rebar-janitor/phases/ticketization.md needs a parallel 'read shared/test-design.md before recording tickets with testing/verification ACs' instruction, or explicitly document why it is excluded (e.g., janitor tickets are maintenance-only and never reference behavioral oracles).
findings.finding: rebar-janitor's Phase 5 (examples/claude-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/ticketization.md) also creates tickets with acceptance criteria — the same trigger condition the plan adds to brainstorm ('before recording any ticket whose ACs include or reference testing, verification, or behavioral oracles, read the shared file'). rebar-janitor is not named as an edit target despite being a ticket-recording skill.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the portability mitigation by adding a brief fallback note at the `../shared/test-design.md` reference point in each SKILL.md (e.g., 'if this file is missing, copy `examples/claude-skills/shared/` alongside this skill directory') so the executing agent has a recovery hint at the exact point of failure, not only in the top-level README.
findings.finding: The plan's 'resolves in a skills installation' claim relies solely on a README note instructing users to copy shared/ alongside skill directories — there is no structural or in-skill mechanism to surface a broken reference to the executing agent at runtime. If a user copies a single skill without shared/, the agent encounters a missing-file error with no contextual recovery hint at the point of failure.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — 'the portability cost of the cross-directory reference is mitigated by an explicit README note'
findings.suggested_fix: At each integration point in the three SKILL.md files, alongside the `../shared/test-design.md` reference, add one sentence: 'If this file is not present, copy the `shared/` directory from `examples/claude-skills/` alongside this skill's directory.' This makes the mitigation self-contained at the point of use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback minimum-viable-RED specification for LLM-surface bugs in `test-design.md`'s eval section (and/or the rebar-debug Step 4 integration note) for when full multi-sample quantified eval is impractical — defining what constitutes an acceptable RED artifact at the minimum bar.
findings.finding: The plan supersedes rebar-debug's Step 4 LLM-surface paragraph with the shared file's stricter quantified-eval standard (pinned model/settings, multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control), but specifies no fallback minimum-viable-RED definition for constrained execution environments where multi-sample evals are impractical (rate limits, cost, no model access). The current SKILL.md provides a workable minimal bar ('an eval or behavioural assertion that fails…'); the plan removes that bar without replacing it with a degraded-mode path.
findings.location: Plan section: Skill integration — rebar-debug Step 4 / 'its LLM-surface paragraph defers to the shared eval standard… superseding its current single-eval description'
findings.suggested_fix: In the shared file's LLM-surface eval section, add a tiered-eval rule: e.g., 'When multi-sample collection is impractical (rate limits, cost, sandboxed environment), the minimum RED artifact is a paired before/after prompt with a single observed failure and a stated expected postcondition — document the constraint and flag the eval as non-quantified. Quantified multi-sample eval is required before the fix ships to production.'

findings.finding: The rebar-brainstorm integration uses a conditional trigger ('when recording tickets that include testing work, read the shared file') whose evaluation is left entirely to the LLM agent's judgment at runtime, with no defined trigger guard. Brainstorm's role is to clarify intent *before* knowing the ticket shape, so the agent may routinely not identify 'testing work' until after the file-read window has passed.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the brainstorm integration to: 'Before recording any ticket whose acceptance criteria will include or reference testing, verification, or behavioral oracles, read the shared file. When in doubt, read it — the cost is a file read, and the benefit is correctly-shaped ACs.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-skill shared/ directory convention (examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md loaded via ../shared/test-design.md) without justifying it against the established per-skill companion-file pattern (e.g., rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md). The existing approach keeps each skill self-contained; the new shared/ pattern creates a cross-directory coupling that breaks skill portability (acknowledged in the README AC but not justified as preferable to duplication or to a per-skill copy).
findings.location: Plan 'Skill integration' section; AC line 'examples/claude-skills/README.md documents the shared/ convention'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the plan's 'Skill integration' or 'What' section, e.g.: 'The shared/ pattern is chosen over per-skill copies because [reason — e.g., a single authoritative source avoids drift across three near-identical copies]; the portability cost is mitigated by the README notice and by the file being small enough to copy manually.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
